segments.s336-g169: از این رای‌گر تاب گیرد دلم is built with the source preposition (از) + noun phrase (این رای‌گر) followed by a verb phrase (تاب گیرد) and the possesive-marked subject (دلم). از here marks origin or cause (“from, because of”), این رای‌گر = “this counsellor/this counsel-giver.” تاب گیرد literally “takes تاب,” where تاب is an abstract noun meaning endurance/patience and گیرد is third-person singular present (or jussive) of گرفتن ‘to take’; the whole verb phrase means “(my) heart takes/receives endurance” or “may my heart be able to endure.” دلم is دل + the 1st-person possessive suffix (-م) = “my heart,” placed after the verb for emphasis (poetic word order).
segments.s336-g170: دل تیره گشته ز تن بگسلم is two coordinated ideas: دل تیره گشته = “the heart has become dark,” where گشته is the perfect/participial form of شدن used as perfect/passive (‘has become’). ز تن بگسلم = “I separate/break from the body,” with ز (poetic form of از) = “from,” تن = “body,” and بگسلم a 1st-person-present/imperfective or jussive form (ب‑ prefix + گسلم) meaning “I break away / I will part.” The clause expresses a result or resolution following the darkening of the heart; the verb form بگسلم carries a volitional/performative sense (“I shall separate”).
segments.s336-g145: ز + با من شدن expresses the means ('by/through becoming with me'); ز is a poetic preposition placed before با. با + من (with me) uses من as object of با. شدن here means 'to become / join' (i.e. 'to join me'). راه کوته کنید is an imperative clause: کوته is an adjective 'short' and کنید is the polite plural imperative of کردن 'to make/causate', so literally 'make the way short.' The verb کندید agrees with an implied plural addressees.
segments.s336-g146: روان را is a definite/accusative-marked object (را marks the direct object). روان here refers metonymically to the travelling party / those in motion (literally 'soul' or 'the moving ones'). سوی روشنی is a prepositional phrase 'toward the light/clarity'; ره کنید again is the causative/imperative 'set on the way / make a course' with کنید as polite plural imperative. Word order places the object (روان را) before the direction phrase and the verb.
segments.s336-g147: بر این ریگ is a locative phrase 'over/on this sand' (بر = on/upon). برنگذرد is an archaic/poetic negative-future/present form meaning 'will not pass' (verb precedes the subject here for emphasis). هر کسی is postposed subject 'anyone/whosoever'; کسی is used in the sense 'anyone.'
segments.s336-g148: مگر introduces an exception ('except, unless'). فره و برز form a coordinated noun phrase 'farrah and barz' (ancient/epic qualities: divine glory and exaltation) that are the objects of دارد ('has'). دارد is present indicative 'has/possesses' and بسی follows as an adverb 'in abundance / much' modifying دارد. The nouns are archaic epic terms functioning as qualities one must possess to pass.
segments.s336-g149: سه precedes the counted noun مرد ('three men'); the adjectival epithets گرانمایه و سرفراز follow the noun (typical Persian order): 'esteemed/precious and proud/victorious.' This whole noun phrase is the subject of the following verbs.
segments.s336-g150: شنیدند is the simple past 3rd-person plural 'they heard' (verb comes first in this clause), with گفتار as its object (no را here). و links the two verbs; گشتند is past 3rd-person plural of گشتن meaning 'they turned / became' and with the adverb باز 'back/again' yields 'they turned back / returned.'
segments.s336-g151: چو at the start is the comparative/simile marker 'like/as.' Here it introduces a list of names: دستان (a proper name in this context, not the common noun 'hands' or 'tales'), و رستم (Rostam), چو again repeats the simile particle before گودرز پیر ('Goudarz the old'). پیر is an epithet 'old/elder' attached to the name. The repetition of چو is a poetic device to list exemplars.
segments.s336-g152: This segment lists epithets that qualify گودرز پیر: جهانجوی ('world-seeking' — an epithet meaning ambitious/seeking the world), بیننده ('beholder/one who sees' — i.e. observant), و یادگیر ('learner/one who takes on learning'). These are nominal epithets placed after the name, joined with و (and), and function as descriptive titles rather than predicates with verbs.
segments.s336-g73: کز introduces a relative clause ('who / that'); ایدر 'here' is adverbial, به + ایوان marks direction 'into/to the iwan', and خرامید is a past 3rd-person singular verb (classical -ید ending) meaning 'strode/strutted proudly'. زود is an adverb placed after the verb to qualify it ('quickly'). The whole segment describes someone moving from this place into the porch.
segments.s336-g74: مدارید is a 2nd-person-plural jussive/imperative form meaning 'be gentle / treat kindly' (classical Persian hortative). در دل مرا = 'in my heart' where م is the enclitic possessor 'my' (here listed separately) and را is the definite/object marker attached for emphasis; جز درود 'except a greeting' specifies what should be allowed into that heart. Word order places the verb before the prepositional phrase, typical of Persian poetic diction.
segments.s336-g75: مباشید is the 2nd-person-plural negative imperative 'do not be'. گستاخ is a predicative adjective 'insolent/impudent', and با + این جهان ('with/against this world') indicates the target of that attitude. The phrase warns against arrogant behavior toward 'this world.'
segments.s336-g76: که functions causally here ('for/because'); او refers back to 'this world' (anaphoric pronoun), بدتری is a noun/adjective phrase meaning 'worse things' or 'a worse side', and دارد is the present tense 'has/possesses'. اندر نهان is a fixed adverbial phrase 'hidden within/secretly inside'. Together it gives the reason: the world conceals worse things.
segments.s336-g77: مباشید is again the negative imperative/formula 'do not be'; جاوید here means 'everlasting/immortal'. جز introduces an exception ('except'), and راد و شاد are two coordinating adjectives/nouns ('generous / noble' and 'happy / joyful'). The clause advises that 'everlasting' should be limited to those who are generous and joyful.
segments.s336-g78: ز من 'of/from me' sets the topic ('as to me' / 'concerning me'), جز 'except', به نیکی 'with goodness' is the manner phrase, مگیرید is 2nd-person-plural present/subjunctive 'you take/you bear' (here used hortatively), and یاد 'remembrance/memory' is the object. Literally: 'Take/hold no remembrance of me except in goodness' — i.e. 'remember me only kindly.'
segments.s336-g79: همه 'everyone' is the subject, شاد و خرم are coordinated adjectives 'happy and joyful', به ایوان 'to/at the iwan' marks location, and شوید is the 2nd-person-plural imperative/subjunctive 'become / be'. The line calls on the assembled to be cheerful together at the porch.
segments.s336-g80: چو 'when' introduces a temporal clause; رفتن بود is an idiomatic past construction meaning 'it was time to go / when going came', where بود is past 'was' and رفتن names the action. The main clause شاد و خندان شوید repeats the hortative 'be happy and smiling' (شوید imperative/subjunctive), giving instructions for departure. The overall sense: when departure comes, leave cheerfully.
segments.s336-g113: چو is a subordinating conjunction meaning 'when' and introduces a temporal clause. خورشید is the subject; بر زد سر is a verbal phrase where زد (literally 'struck') combines with the adverbial/prefix بر to produce the idiom 'lift/raise (one's) head'—سر is the object/personified 'head'. از تیره کوه is a prepositional phrase 'from the dark mountain' (از = 'from', تیره modifying کوه = 'mountain').
segments.s336-g114: بیامد is simple past 'came' (third-person). به پیشش is a directional phrase 'into his presence' (به + پیش with the enclitic -ش = 'his'). ز هر سو گروه is a syntactically postposed source phrase 'a group/troop from every side' (ز = 'from', هر سو = 'every side', گروه = 'troop/company') that completes the motion expressed by بیامد.
segments.s336-g115: زن و مرد ایرانیان is a coordinated noun phrase 'women and men of the Iranians' (ایرانیان in the genitive group). The numeral صدهزار follows the noun phrase and quantifies it (in Persian the numeral can appear after the counted noun), so the whole unit reads 'a hundred thousand Iranian men and women.'
segments.s336-g116: خروشان is a participial/adjectival form 'roaring' or 'clamorous', describing the people; برفتند is the past tense verb 'they went/marched' (3pl). با شهریار is a prepositional phrase 'with the king' indicating accompaniment (با = 'with', شهریار = 'king').
segments.s336-g117: همه کوه is a topical noun phrase 'the whole mountain' (همه used as 'whole/entire'). پر ناله is a predicate 'full of moaning' (پر = 'full (of)', ناله = 'moan/lament'); و با خروش links and adds the manner 'and with a roar' (با خروش = 'with a cry/roar'), preparing the following clause.
segments.s336-g118: همی before the verb is an archaic/poetic progressive/inchoative marker (here giving a vivid ongoing or incipient action). سنگ خارا is the subject 'the flinty/hard stone' (خارا as adjective modifying سنگ). برآمد is past 'arose/issued forth', and به جوش is a prepositional phrase meaning 'into a boil/surge' (به = 'into/toward', جوش = 'boiling/surge'), i.e. 'surged up'.
segments.s336-g119: همی گفت is an iterative/imperfective past sense 'kept saying' (همی signaling repeated action, گفت = 'said'); هر کس is the subject 'everyone/each person'. که introduces the reported question clause; شاها is the vocative 'O king', and چه بود is the interrogative 'what was (it)?' (past copula بود used in the question).
segments.s336-g120: که here introduces the content/result clause ('that...'). روشن functions as a predicate adjective 'bright/clear' placed before the possessed noun; دلت is دل + enclitic -ت 'your heart'. شد is 'became' (past of شدن). پر از introduces the phrase 'full of' (پر = 'full', از = 'of/from'), and داغ و دود list what it is full of: داغ here is the idiomatic 'burning grief' and دود is 'smoke'.
segments.s336-g1: به ایرانیان is a prepositional phrase meaning 'to the Iranians' (به + plural noun). گفت is the past tense verb 'said'; in this poetic line the verb precedes the subject (گفت ... شاه), so پیروز شاه (Pirooz the king) follows the verb as the subject. This inverted order (verb before subject) is common in verse.
segments.s336-g2: که introduces a reported clause ('that' / 'that ...'); بدرود باد is an optative idiom: بدرود 'farewell' + باد (optative particle 'may it be'), so together 'may ... be farewell'. این دل‌افروز گاه is the noun phrase 'this heart‑cheering/delightful time/hour' (demonstrative + adjective + noun).
segments.s336-g3: چو here means 'when'; it introduces a temporal clause. The clause has subject من 'I' and the verb بگذرم (present/subjunctive form of گذشتن) used with a temporal sense 'when I pass / when I leave'. ز این فرومایه خاک is a prepositional noun phrase 'from this lowly soil/earth' (ز = from, then demonstrative + adjective + noun).
segments.s336-g4: شما را places the object 'you' with the object marker را before the verb (typical in Persian). بخواهم is first‑person present/subjunctive used here with future or volitional force ('I will ask / I shall beseech'). ز یزدان پاک is the source/agent phrase 'of/from the Pure God' (meaning 'I will ask the Pure God on your behalf').
segments.s336-g5: به پدرود کردن is a purpose/temporal gerund phrase 'in bidding farewell / when taking leave' (به + infinitive/gerund). رخ هر کسی is the noun phrase 'the face of each person' (رخ = face, هر کسی = every person); the whole phrase expresses the circumstance in which the action occurs.
segments.s336-g6: ببوسید is the simple past (3rd singular) 'he kissed'. با آب مژگان بسی is an instrumental/modal phrase 'with the water of his eyelashes (i.e. with tears on his lashes)' where بسی (an adverb) intensifies quantity ('plentifully' / 'much'). The order places the manner phrase after the verb, a normal placement.
segments.s336-g7: یلان را: یلان (heroes/champions) is marked with the object/topic marker را; همه 'all' + پاک 'completely/wholly' modify the group and the manner. در بر گرفت is an idiomatic compound 'to take into the arms' = 'to embrace' (در + بر + گرفت combine to form the verbal phrase). The verb گرفت is past tense 'took/seized'.
segments.s336-g8: به زاری 'with lamentation' indicates the manner. خروشیدن is the infinitive 'to cry out / to wail'; اندر گرفت is a verbal phrase in past tense meaning 'they began to' or literally 'took to' (here 'began to wail'). So the structure is manner phrase + infinitive + past verb to mean 'they began wailing in lamentation.'
segments.s336-g33: که introduces a causal/explanatory clause ('for'/'because'). رفتیم is past tense, first-person plural 'we went/left'. اینک is an adverb 'now' placed after the verb for emphasis. ز + جای = 'from the place'; سپنج is a poetic/archaic noun ('sponge' or cushioned pad) modifying جای, so ز جای سپنج = 'from the cushioned place'.
segments.s336-g34: شما is the 2nd‑person plural/formal pronoun 'you'. دل مدارید is an exhortation: مداریدن here means 'treat gently / spare' and is directed at the addressee's heart (دل) — i.e. 'spare your hearts'. با introduces the manner/cause 'with' and درد و رنج lists the things to be spared: 'pain and sorrow/suffering'.
segments.s336-g35: نبینید is a jussive/optative/negative verb form here meaning 'may you not see' / 'do not see' (used as a wish or prohibition). جاوید functions adverbially as 'ever/evermore'. ز این پس = 'from this after' = 'henceforth'. م + را together mark the object 'me' (object clitic + object marker); the line presents the object after the temporal phrase.
segments.s336-g36: کز begins a causal/explanatory subordinate clause here ('for / from which / because'). این خاک = 'this earth/soil', and بیدادگر is an adjective 'cruel, oppressive' describing that earth. بس means 'enough' (i.e. 'has had enough of me' or 'is satisfied with me'). م + را again mark the object 'me' (clitic + object marker).
segments.s336-g37: سوی is the preposition 'toward/unto'. داور پاک is an epithet 'the Pure Judge' (here a divine/judicial figure). خواهم is the auxiliary/verb 'I will / I desire' and is followed by the infinitive شدن ('to become / to go / to be made'); together they express the speaker's intention or future action: 'I will go/be brought before the Pure Judge.' Word order is somewhat poetic (preposition + object before the verbal phrase).
segments.s336-g38: نبینم is first‑person negative present 'I do not / I see not' (here: 'I see no...'). همی is an emphatic/adverbial particle ('indeed / at all / any longer' depending on context) that strengthens the negative. راه بازآمدن is a noun phrase 'the way of returning' (راه + infinitive بازآمدن = 'the path/possibility of coming back'), the object of نبینم: 'I see no way to return.'
segments.s336-g39: بشد is a past/formal perfective 'it became / it happened' used here with هوش ('awareness, sense') to mean 'their senses were roused' or 'awareness came'. زان = 'from that / thereupon' connects this result to what preceded. چار خورشید چهر is a descriptive noun group: چار = 'four', خورشید چهر = 'sun‑faced' (literally 'sun‑face', i.e. faces like the sun); together 'those four with sun‑like faces'. Word order places the numeral and epithet before the head noun.
segments.s336-g40: خروشان شد(ند) is a compound: خروشان (roaring/tumultuous) + شدند (they became) = 'they became tumultuous/roaring'. از introduces the cause ('because of / from') and the list غم و درد و مهر gives the emotions: غم 'grief/sorrow', درد 'pain/suffering', مهر 'love/affection'. The whole clause reads 'they became tumultuous from grief, pain, and love.'
segments.s336-g105: همی + past verb here expresses repeated/continuous past action ('kept going'). لشکر is the subject placed before the verb (classical word order is flexible). گروهاگروه is a reduplicated adverbial phrase modifying the motion, meaning 'in groups / one after another.'
segments.s336-g106: ز هامون (prepositional phrase) marks origin ('from the plain'); بشد is the classical perfective/past of شدن used here to mean 'came' or 'came to be/appeared' (transitive sense is not intended). تا introduces the extent 'as far as'; سر تیغ کوه is a genitive phrase 'the summit/ridge of the mountain' (سر = summit, تیغ = ridge/point).
segments.s336-g107: ببودند is the past plural 'they were / they remained'; یک هفته is a duration complement ('for a week') following the verb. دم بر زدند is an idiomatic verb phrase: دم = 'breath' and بر زدند ('struck upon') together convey 'drew breath / paused'.
segments.s336-g108: یکی here functions adverbially ('once' / 'at one time' or 'one of them'); بر لب خشک is a locative phrase 'on the dry brink' (بر marks location), and نم بر زدند is again 'a drop of moisture struck/appeared' with زدند as the past verb. The repeated بر first marks location (بر لب) and then combines with زدند in the idiom 'نم بر زدند.'
segments.s336-g109: خروشان و جوشان are present-participle/adjectival forms ('roaring and seething') describing the scene or people; ز before کردار شاه marks cause ('because of the king's action/deeds'). Word order places the adjectival phrases up front for emphasis (poetic style).
segments.s336-g110: کسی را نبود is an existential negative construction: کسی را (with the object/topic marker را) + نبود ('there was not') yields 'there was no one.' اندر آن رنج راه is a locative/adverbial phrase 'in that hardship/arduous road.' Note that را is used here even with an indefinite word in a classical poetic style.
segments.s336-g111: همی گفت is iterative/progressive ('kept saying' or 'would say repeatedly'). هر موبدی is the subject 'every/each priest (mobad)'. در نهفت is a fixed adverbial phrase meaning 'in secret' or 'in concealment' (نهفت = hidden). Note: the provided senses list for گفت does not include the verb sense 'said', so its sense index is marked -1.
segments.s336-g112: کز introduces a reported clause ('that'); این‌سان means 'thus' or 'in this manner'; همی here emphasizes 'ever/at any time' in the negative clause. در جهان is 'in the world' and کس نگفت means 'no one said' (کس used in negative contexts = 'no one'). The verb نگفت is the negative past 'did not say' — the provided sense list for نگفت likewise does not include a verb sense, so it is indexed -1.
segments.s336-g81: This is a noun phrase with quantifier + noun + genitive + noun-object before the verb: همه (a universal quantifier) precedes نامداران (the noun ‘renowned ones’). ایران is a post‑nominal modifier (‘of Iran’). سپاه follows as the object/role word (‘a host, an army’) and here belongs to the whole subject phrase that will be linked to the verb in the next segment. Note Persian often places such objects/hosts before the verb.
segments.s336-g82: نهادند is the 3rd person plural past of نهادن 'to place/establish' and functions as the main verb 'they put/assembled'. سر بر زمین is a compound prepositional phrase ('head upon the ground') meaning they prostrated or laid their heads on the ground; بر is the preposition 'on/upon' and زمین is 'ground'. پیش شاه places the action 'in the presence of the king' (پیش = 'before/in the presence of', شاه = 'king').
segments.s336-g83: که introduces a content clause ('that'), ما is the subject 'we'. پند او را is the definite object phrase 'his counsel/advice' (او = 'his', را = object marker). به کردار جان is a simile phrase 'in the manner/esteem of life' (به = 'as/to', کردار here = 'conduct/guise' or 'regard', جان = 'life'); together it means 'regard his advice as you would regard life.'
segments.s336-g84: بداریم is the 1st plural subjunctive/optative form meaning 'we (should) hold/keep' (here 'hold as if life'). تا introduces the purpose/result clause 'so that/that' and governs جان بود جاودان — جان 'life', بود 'be' (here optative/subjunctive sense 'may be'), جاودان 'eternal'. The whole segment expresses purpose: we shall keep his counsel so that life may remain eternal.
segments.s336-g85: به + personal name marks the indirect object/recipient ('to Lohrasp'). فرمود is an honorific/polite past verb 'he said/commanded'. تا + بازگشت here introduces the subordinate infinitival/intent clause meaning 'to return' or 'that he return' (تا with a verb gives purpose/command: 'in order that he return').
segments.s336-g86: بدو is a clitic prepositional pronoun 'to him' (به + او contracted). گفت is the past-tense verb 'said' (here introducing direct speech). روز من اندر گذشت is an idiomatic clause 'my day has passed' (روز = 'day/time', من = 'my/I', اندر گذشت = 'passed/has gone by'), meaning his time/fortune is over. The order is typical Persian: indirect object + verb + reported clause.
segments.s336-g87: This is an address plus imperatives. تو 'you' (vocative/subject), رو is an imperative/verb meaning 'go/take (a seat)' or 'turn' (classical imperative of رفتن), تخت شاهی is the noun phrase 'royal throne' (تخت = throne, شاهی = royal/kingship), and به‑آیین بدار are a prepositional adverbial + verb meaning 'keep/maintain with proper ritual/decency' (به‑آیین = 'with propriety', بدار functioning as an imperative 'hold/keep'). Together: 'You, take the royal throne and hold it with proper decorum.'
segments.s336-g88: به گیتی = 'in the world' (به = 'in/to', گیتی = 'the world'). جز از = 'except from' or 'except in/other than'. تخم نیکی = 'the seed/origin of goodness' (تخم = 'seed/origin', نیکی = 'goodness/good deed'). مکار at the end is an admonition 'do not be deceitful' or a negative injunction; classically it functions as a warning against deceit. The whole line reads as a moral injunction: in the world do not be deceitful except where it stems from goodness.
segments.s336-g161: This is an inverted, somewhat archaic word order: the indirect object 'بدان' (to those) and the addressee 'مرزبانان' come before the verb phrase. 'بدان' here is a poetic form meaning 'به آن/به آن‌ها' (to those); 'چنین' is an adverb 'thus/so' modifying the reported speech; the verb 'گفت' (said) follows and the speaker 'شاه' (the king) appears after the verb for emphasis—i.e. 'to those marzbans the king said thus'.
segments.s336-g162: 'که' introduces the content of what was said (a subordinate/that‑clause). 'امشب' = 'tonight'. 'نرانیم' is first‑person plural with the negative prefix ن‑ (here jussive/optative/present sense): 'we will not / let us not drive/move (away)'; in context it means 'we will not leave/move from'. 'ز' = poetic 'from', and 'این جایگاه' = 'this place/position'. The clause as a whole reports the king's sentence: 'that tonight we shall not depart from this place.'
segments.s336-g163: 'بجوییم' is a first‑person plural jussive/subjunctive form of 'جستن/جستن' meaning 'let us seek' or 'we will seek' (poetic imperatival sense). The direct object 'کار گذشته' ('deeds/affairs of the past') follows the verb, and 'بسی' is an adverb 'bisy' = 'much/ample/thoroughly' modifying the search.
segments.s336-g164: 'کز' (= 'که از/از این‌رو که') introduces the result or purposive clause: 'that/from this on...'. 'پس' here in the fixed phrase 'از این پس' means 'after/ henceforth'. 'نبینند' is a negative subjunctive/optative form (3rd plural) 'they should not see' or 'will not see'; Persian often places the verb before the subject for emphasis, so 'نبینند ما را کسی' literally 'may no one see us' = 'no one will see us.' 'ما را' is the object ('us') marked by 'را'; 'کسی' here, under negation, means 'no one'.
segments.s336-g165: 'چو' is a temporal conjunction here ('when'). 'خورشید تابان' is the noun plus postpositive adjective ('the shining/radiant sun'). 'برآرد' is a poetic 3rd‑person singular present/future of 'برآوردن' meaning 'raises/puts up' (here 'raises'). 'درفش' (banner/standard) is the object placed after the verb, so the clause reads 'when the radiant sun raises the standard.'
segments.s336-g166: Again 'چو' = 'when'. 'زر آب گردد' is a compact poetic phrase: 'زر' = 'gold' and 'آب گردد' = 'becomes water/turns to water' or 'flows like water' (the verb 'گردد' = 'become' in a present/poetic form). The clause ends with 'زمین بنفش' = 'the earth becomes/presents purple' (بنفش = purple). Grammatically this is a pair of temporal clauses joined by repeated 'چو' describing a dawning or color transformation.
segments.s336-g167: Literary word order: 'مرا روزگار جدایی بود' literally 'to me a time of separation was' — i.e. 'my days were a time of separation' or 'I had days of separation.' Here 'مرا' functions as a dative/topic ('for me'); 'روزگار' = 'time/fate', 'جدایی' = 'separation', and 'بود' is the past copula 'was.' Note the older/poetic placement and splitting of the pronoun (surface tokens 'م' + 'را').
segments.s336-g168: 'مگر' introduces an exception ('except'/'except that'). 'با سروش آشنایی بود' is a simple clause: 'with Soroush there was familiarity' = 'I had acquaintance/familiarity with Soroush.' 'سروش' here is the divine messenger/angelic figure; 'آشنایی' = 'acquaintance, familiar contact'; 'بود' is the past copula 'was'.
segments.s336-g97: بدو is the prepositional/dative form 'to him' (be‑ + third‑person), placed before the verb. گفت is the past tense verb 'said' (3rd sg.); خسرو is the subject placed after the verb (typical epic word order: indirect object + verb + subject). که introduces the following clause as a content clause. پدرود باش is an optative/imperative formula 'be (a) farewell' or 'farewell, be' (باش is the jussive/imperative).
segments.s336-g98: This is an imperative-style instruction introduced by the preposition به 'to/for'. داد is a noun here meaning 'justice' or 'justness' and اندرون 'inside/inner (apartments)' specifies the place; the phrase behaves like a predicative slot ('be just in the inner apartment'). تارگر and پود are nouns placed in sequence (two roles/qualities) and باش is the imperative 'be' applying to the preceding descriptors.
segments.s336-g99: برفتند is the past‑plural verb 'they departed/they went'; it appears first (verb‑initial order). با او is the prepositional phrase 'with him' (company); ز ایران is another prepositional phrase 'from Iran'. سران is the logical subject/phrase listing who went ('the chiefs').
segments.s336-g100: This is a noun phrase further describing the people who went: بزرگان 'the great ones' is the head noun, followed by adjectival/participial modifiers (بیدار 'awake/alert') linked by و 'and' to another plural noun (کنداوران). Persian places modifiers after the noun; the whole phrase is in apposition to سران above.
segments.s336-g101: چو is a comparative/simile particle 'as/like' introducing a list of names. The sequence here lists pairs (چو X و Y) — the particle repeats before the second pair to mean 'such as X and Y, such as A and B.' Word order simply places names after چو, joined by و.
segments.s336-g102: دگر functions as 'other/another' or 'besides/also' introducing additional names. The following sequence is a bare list of proper names connected by و; there is no copula — simple nominal listing is common in epic catalogs. Some proper names here use archaic or variant spellings.
segments.s336-g103: به هفتم is an adverbial/locative phrase 'to the seventh' or 'in the seventh (place)'. فریبرز and کاووس are proper names placed together (apposition or genitival association typical in lists), and بود is the past copula 'was' (3rd sg.). The phrase states who held the seventh place.
segments.s336-g104: Similarly, به هشتم 'to/in the eighth' introduces the next place; کجا appears here as a particle/connector (literally 'where' in the lexicon given but functioning in the line as part of the phrase), نامور is an adjective 'renowned', طوس is the proper name 'Tous', and بود is the past copula 'was'. Together they state who occupied the eighth position.
segments.s336-g17: به ایرانیان آن زمان is an indirect-object prepositional phrase ('to the Iranians of that time'). گفت is the past tense verb 'said'. شاه is the subject placed after the verb (a common poetic inversion: verb before subject).
segments.s336-g18: که introduces the reported content ('that'). فردا is a temporal adverb 'tomorrow'. شما را uses را after the pronoun to mark the addressee/oblique object ('for you' / 'to you'). همین است راه is a poetic word order: copula است stands between the demonstrative همین and the noun راه (more prosaically: 'این راه است' → 'this is the way').
segments.s336-g19: هر آن کس که is a fixed relative phrase 'whoever / every one who'. دارید is a present-tense verb 'have/possess' used here in classical/honorific style to form the relative clause 'who possesses'. نام و نژاد are the two coordinated objects 'name and lineage'.
segments.s336-g20: به before دادار خورشید indicates 'in/with/regard to' the divine figure; دادار خورشید is an epithet (the Creator / the Sun). باشید is the 2nd-person plural subjunctive/optative 'may you be' (a blessing formula). شاد is the predicative adjective 'happy/joyful' following باشیـد.
segments.s336-g21: من اکنون sets the subject and time 'I now'. روان را uses را to mark روان as the definite direct object 'my soul' (object marker without a possessive pronoun is idiomatic in poetry). همی is an archaic/progressive particle marking continuous or habitual action; پرورم is 1st-person-present 'I nurture/cherish'.
segments.s336-g22: که here means 'so that / in order that' introducing purpose. بر نیک‌نامی uses بر + noun to indicate movement/toward or attainment ('to/into good repute'). مگر is an archaic modal particle expressing a shade of hope/possibility ('perhaps' / 'that I may'), and بگذرم is the verb 'I may pass/attain' (subjunctive/optative sense following the modal).
segments.s336-g23: نبستم is past negative 'I did not close/bind' (نـ- prefix + past stem). دل is the direct object 'heart' (no را is required in poetry). اندر is the preposition 'in/within'. سپنجی سرای is a descriptive noun phrase 'a sponge-like chamber/house' (سپنجی = 'spongy', سرای = 'chamber/house' here).
segments.s336-g24: بدان is a fused expression meaning 'for that / to that end' (ب- prefix + دان); تا introduces a temporal boundary 'until'. سروش is the divine messenger/angel. آمدم is past 'I came' (here 'came' in the sense 'arrived'). رهنمای is the predicate noun 'a guide' (the srowsh came as my guide). Word order is somewhat inverted in poetry: the verb آمدم appears before the predicate noun رهنمای.
segments.s336-g129: همه (all/everyone) is the actor/topic placed at the front for emphasis. پیش یزدان = “in the presence of God” (پیش here = 'in the presence of') and follows the actor. ستایش is a noun 'praise/worship' and کنیم is a first‑person plural present-subjunctive/hortative verb ('let us ... / we should ...') placed at the end, so the whole clause reads literally ‘all before God praise let us (do)’.
segments.s336-g130: به آتشکده is a locative/directional phrase ('to/at the fire‑temple'); در here marks the internal locus ('in/inside') and combines with نیایش ('prayer') so that در نیایش کنيم = 'let us pray inside/at the fire‑temple.' کنیم again is the hortative/subjunctive verb that closes the clause.
segments.s336-g131: مگر here introduces a wish/condition (classical use: 'if only' / 'may') tied to the following verb. پاک qualifies یزدانت ('your God' — یزدان + possessive suffix -ت). بخشد is a third‑person verb meaning 'give/bestow' and is used optatively here ('may ... bestow'). به ما is the recipient phrase 'to us.'
segments.s336-g132: دل موبدان = 'the hearts of the mobeds' (possessive relation by juxtaposition; classical Persian often omits the ezāfe). بردرخشد is a single verbal unit meaning 'shine upon/beam on' (third person, here optative/expressing hope: 'may ... shine on'). به ما is 'upon us' / 'to us.'
segments.s336-g133: شهنشاه is the subject 'the king/emperor.' زآن (ز + آن) = 'from/because of that' links to کار ('deed/act'), so زآن کار = 'by/at that deed.' خیره بماند is a light verb construction meaning 'remained astonished' — بماند 'remained' at the end is the main verb in narrative position.
segments.s336-g134: از آن انجمن = 'from that assembly.' موبدان را is the direct object with the topical/definite marker را. بخواند is the verb 'he called/summoned' (here past/narrative third person), so the clause means 'he called/summoned the mobeds from that assembly.'
segments.s336-g135: چنین is an adverb 'thus/so.' گفت here functions as the reporting verb 'said' (the printed senses list does not include 'said', so that sense is the verbal use in context). ایدر = 'here.' همه نیکویست is a verbless clause where نیکویست (نیکویی + است) contains the copula -ست = 'is good/excellent,' so 'everything here is good.'
segments.s336-g136: بر این نیکویها = 'about/over these good (things)' with بر marking the object of emotion. نباید is an impersonal modal 'one must not / it should not be' and is followed by گریست (the verbal form 'to weep/weep') to express prohibition: 'one should not weep.' In classical usage the past form گریست is used where modern Persian would use کند or the infinitive.
segments.s336-g137: ز + یزدان + شناسید is addressing or describing the addressees as ‘those who know God’. Here شناسید is 2nd person plural (you know / you are knowers) used as a descriptive predicate; ز is the poetic preposition 'from/because/of' that introduces the ground or characteristic (literally 'of God‑knowing'). یکسر (an adverb) modifies سپاس, meaning 'entirely/wholly'. Word order is inverted for poetic effect: literally 'of God‑knowing you are, entirely praise'.
segments.s336-g138: مباشید is a negative jussive/imperative form meaning 'do not be' / 'may you not be'. It governs the remainder 'جز پاک یزدان‑شناس' (= 'except a pure God‑knower'), so the whole clause means 'be nothing except a pure God‑knower'. جز is the preposition 'except', پاک is the adjective 'pure', and یزدان‑شناس is the substantive epithet addressed to the listener.
segments.s336-g139: که introduces a causal/explanatory clause ('for/since'). گرد آمدن is a deverbal noun phrase 'coming together, gathering' (گرد here carries the sense 'around/together'), زود is an adverb 'soon', and باشد is the copula used here to assert/future‑expectation ('will be' / 'is likely to be'). به هم (literally 'to/toward one another') specifies that the coming is mutual—'be gathered together soon'.
segments.s336-g140: مباشید is the negative imperative/jussive 'do not be'. ز این رفتن من is an ablative/explanatory phrase 'because of this my going/leave' (ز = 'because of/from', این = 'this', رفتن = 'the going/leave', من = 'my'), and دژم is a predicative adjective 'sullen/grim'. Together: 'Do not be sullen because of this my departure.'
segments.s336-g141: بدان here is an archaic fused prepositional phrase (به + آن) meaning 'to those' or 'to that/these', introducing the indirect object 'مهتران' ('the chiefs/elders'). گفت is the past tense verb 'he said'. ز این کوهسار is a prepositional phrase 'from/on this mountain' indicating location or the speaker's position. Word order is typical for narrative: indirect object + گفت + locative phrase.
segments.s336-g142: همه is an address 'all (of you)'; بازگردید is the 2nd person plural imperative 'return!' (back to where you came from); بی‑شهریار is a vocative epithet 'O kingless one' / 'O without ruler' appended to the command as an address to the addressee. The clause is a direct imperative order.
segments.s336-g143: که again introduces an explanatory clause ('for/that'). راهی here functions as 'a road / the route' (poetic form), دراز است means 'is long'. The coordinated adjectives 'بی‑آب و سخت' follow, describing the road as 'without water and difficult/harsh'. The copula است links the subject phrase to its adjectival description.
segments.s336-g144: نباشد here functions as a negative existential/capability clause meaning 'there will not be / there is not' (classical use), governing the contrastive list 'گیاه و نه برگ درخت' = 'plant life, nor even leaves of trees'. نه before برگ intensifies the negation ('nor'). The phrase states there will be no vegetation nor leaves on trees along that road.
segments.s336-g41: شخودند is a poetic past/plural form meaning 'they were proud/they boasted' (here: 'they humbled their pride' in sense of giving up pride). The object ما/را is not present, so the clause attaches to the following action; روی ('face') follows شخودند to indicate the locus (their faces). The sequence 'روی و بکندند موی' uses the conjunction و to link 'face' with the next verb phrase, where بکندند is a past plural verb 'they pulled/plucked' (imperative-root form with past plural ending) and موی is its object placed after the verb—a common poetic inversion.
segments.s336-g42: گسستند is a past plural verb 'they broke/tore off' (here used transitively with the following nouns). پیرایه, رنگ, و بوی are a tersely listed direct objects (ornament, color/appearance, and scent), linked by و. The overall structure is paratactic: successive past verbs describing physical signs of mourning or renunciation.
segments.s336-g43: از آن پس is a temporal phrase 'from that time on / thereafter' (پس here = 'after'). هر آن کس که آمد به هوش is a relative clause 'whoever came to awareness' (آمد is past 'came' used idiomatically for 'regained'; به هوش = 'to awareness'). Word order is standard Persian: temporal phrase + quantifier + relative clause.
segments.s336-g44: چنین گفت is a fixed poetic formula 'said thus' (گفت here functions as the past-tense verb 'said' even though the supplied senses list gives noun senses; treat it as the verb). با ناله و با خروش 'with moaning and with clamour' uses repeated با for rhythmic emphasis and two nouns coordinated by و. The whole clause describes how the speech was delivered.
segments.s336-g45: که introduces direct speech here ('that' / 'that is, they said'). ما را ببر is an object-before-verb construction with را marking the definite object 'us' and ببر as an imperative 'take!' (note the homograph بُبر 'tiger' but context makes it an imperative). ز این سرای سپنج is a prepositional phrase 'from this سرای (here described by سپنج, 'sponge' or 'sponge-like'),' with ز (poetic) = 'from.'
segments.s336-g46: رها کن تو ما را is a second imperative: رها کن = 'set (them) free' or 'release' (کن is the imperative 'do'), here addressed to 'تو' (you); ما را is again the object with the را marker. از این درد و رنج is a standard from-phrase 'from this pain and suffering' (درد here carries emotional-sorrow sense). The sequence gives a plea in two imperative clauses.
segments.s336-g47: بدیشان ('to them') is a prepositional/dative phrase placed up front for emphasis. چنین گفت 'said thus' again is a formulaic verb phrase; the subject پر مایه شاه ('the king, full of resources') comes after گفت by poetic inversion (English would more naturally place the subject before the verb). پر مایه is a compound adjective: پر 'full (of)' + مایه 'resource/substance'.
segments.s336-g48: کز این پس is a fused form ('that from this time on' = 'from now on'). شما را functions as a dative/beneficiary with the را marker ('for you'), and the predicate همین است راه is an inverted copular clause literally 'this is the way' but arranged so that English reads 'this is your way': همین 'this', است 'is', راه 'way/path'. The word order (شما را before the predicate) is a standard Persian stylistic order: 'for you is this the way.'
segments.s336-g153: نگشتند is the past negative of گشتن (they did not turn/return); the verb comes first (poetic word-order) and the subject is implied ‘they’. ز او is a prepositional phrase 'from him' (z = from, او = him/her). باز is an adverb ‘back/again’ modifying the verb. چون introduces a comparison 'like' and links to the following names طوس و گیو.
segments.s336-g154: همان is an emphatic determiner here 'that very' or 'the same' placed before the proper name بیژن. و هم uses و = 'and' and هم = 'also' (adding the next name). فریبرز is a personal name; نیو here functions as part of the name (poetic/onomastic element) rather than the literal senses listed.
segments.s336-g155: برفتند is past-tense plural 'they went / set out' (بر‑ prefixed verb). یک روز و یک شب is a time phrase 'one day and one night' placed after the verb. به هم is an adverbial phrase meaning 'together' (به + هم; be‑ here functions as a prepositional/relational element and هم = together).
segments.s336-g156: شدند is the past plural of شدن; here it functions as the simple past intransitive 'they came/appeared/issued forth' (poetic usage). از introduces the origin 'from/out of', modifying بیابان 'desert/wilderness'. و links خشکی 'dry land' which is qualified by the postpositive adjective دژم 'sullen/grim' (adjective after noun is common in older Persian).
segments.s336-g157: به ره is a prepositional phrase 'on the way / along the road' (به = to/towards/on, ره = way). بر یکی چشمه literally 'upon a certain spring' (بر = on/upon; یکی = one/a certain; چشمه = spring). آمد پدید is a two-word verb phrase 'came forth / appeared' (آمد = came, پدید = manifest/appeared); the adjective/verb پدید follows آمد in poetic order but together mean 'appeared'.
segments.s336-g158: جهانجوی is an epithet 'world‑seeking' placed before the proper name کیخسرو (a stock epithet in the Shahnameh). آنجا 'there' marks the place, and رسید is the past 'he arrived / reached' (third person past of رسیدن). Word order is epithet + name + locative + verb (common in epic diction).
segments.s336-g159: بدان is the contracted form of به آن 'to that' (poetic fusion: به + آن) introducing the place; here the dataset splits it as ب + دان, but syntactically it functions as the prepositional phrase 'to that'. آب روشن = 'clear/bright water' (noun + postpositive adjective). فرود آمدند is a compound verbal phrase 'they alighted/descended' (فرود = descent/alighting, آمدند = they came).
segments.s336-g160: بخوردند is past plural of خوردن 'they ate/drank' (here likely 'they drank' at the spring). چیزی 'something' is the direct object. و = 'and' links the next clause. دم بر زدند is a light-verb idiom: دم = 'breath/respiration' and بر زدند (زدند = they struck/sounded) together mean 'they drew a breath / gave a gasp / made a sound' (an idiomatic, poetic expression); literally 'they struck a breath upon (themselves)'.
segments.s336-g57: مجویید is an archaic/poetic negative imperative (2nd-person plural) meaning “do not seek.” از این رفتن is a prepositional phrase where از marks source/means (“by / because of this going”); رفتن is a verbal noun (“the going/departure”) and آزار من is the affected thing (“my harm / my suffering”), with the whole phrase functioning as the object/goal of مجویید.
segments.s336-g58: که introduces a subordinate (purpose/result) clause here: “so that / in order that.” شود is the classical 3rd-person singular present/subjunctive of شدن used as a permissive/optative (“may become / will become”). راه دشوار من is the noun phrase “my difficult way/road,” with دشوار as the adjective after the noun (typical Persian order: noun + adjective) and من marking possession.
segments.s336-g59: خروشید is a perfective/past verb meaning “he roared/cried out.” The coordinating و links that clause to the next action. لهراسب را پیش خواند is a transitive past clause: را marks لهراسب as definite object, پیش is adverbial (“forward / before [the assembly] / into the presence of”), and خواند is past tense “he called / he summoned.”
segments.s336-g60: از ایشان here uses از with a source sense (“from / out of them”): the clause says he elicited or drew words from those people. فراوان سخنها is the noun phrase “many / abundant words,” and براند is an older perfective 3rd-person form of راندن/راند (literally “drove / drove forth”); used figuratively here it means he caused/elicited many words (i.e. many utterances) from them.
segments.s336-g61: به لهراسب گفت is a simple past clause: به marks the indirect object/recipient (“to Lohrasp”) and گفت is the past-tense verb “he said.” این بتان is the nominal subject (“these idols”). منند is an archaic/formal copular or predicative form here (classical usage) linking subject to possession/predicate — in effect functioning as “are mine” / “belong to me” in context.
segments.s336-g62: فروزنده is an adjectival participle “radiant / illuminating” modifying the subject (the idols). پاک جان is a tight adjective + noun phrase meaning “pure of soul” (پاک qualifying جان), and the final منند again functions as the archaic copula/predicate marker agreeing with the plural subject (i.e. “they are”). The whole segment is a nominal predicate describing the idols.
segments.s336-g63: بر این هم نشست is a past action: بر (on/upon) + این هم (this same / here also) with نشست (sat/settled) as the verb. اندر این هم سرای places the action spatially: اندر = in/within, این هم سرای = “this very house/abode” (هم adding the sense ‘same/also’), so the clause says he took his place there in that same house.
segments.s336-g64: همی here is an adverbial particle giving continuity/ongoing sense (“so / thereby / kept thus” or “still”), and دارشان is a composite showing possession/object (here functioning as ‘kept them / put them’ — in classical usage دار + pronominal suffix –شان can indicate keeping/placing ‘them’). تا introduces the temporal limit “until,” باشی is a subjunctive/present form meaning “you may be / you should be,” and به جای means “in place / in your post.” Together: he kept them there until you should be in your place.
segments.s336-g49: کجا is the interrogative adverb 'where' placed before the noun phrase to form a rhetorical question. خواهران is the plural noun 'sisters'; جهاندار is an epithet 'world‑holder/sovereign' that qualifies جم (Jam) which follows as the name being described (appositive/genitive relation). There is no overt copula—this is an elliptical question typical of epic poetry.
segments.s336-g50: Again کجا begins the rhetorical question. تاجداران is a plural agentive 'crown‑bearers' (those who wear the crown). با is the preposition 'with'; باد و دم are nouns 'wind' and 'breath' used together idiomatically to indicate transience/instantaneity (they are presented as the things with which these rulers have gone). The verb is omitted — a poetic ellipsis.
segments.s336-g51: کجا is the interrogative 'where'. مادرم is 'my mother' (مادر + the possessive suffix -م); the segment is listed as two tokens (مادر + م) in the data, but here the combined form functions as 'my mother' (possessive). دخت افراسیاب is an appositive 'daughter of Afrasiab' describing that mother. The phrase is elliptical (no copula).
segments.s336-g52: که introduces a relative clause 'who/that'. بگذشت is past/preterite 'passed/by' used here in the sense of 'surpassed/went beyond'. زآن سان is a frozen phrase meaning 'to/than that degree' or 'in that way' (ز + آن + سان). بدریای آب is a compound/name: بدریای functions as a proper or poetic name (Badrīyā) linked to آب 'water, the sea' — together a poetic comparison of beauty. The clause modifies the mother.
segments.s336-g53: کجا opens the question. دختر تور is 'the daughter of Tur' (noun + genitive/possession). ماه آفرید is a poetic compound: ماه 'moon' + آفرید 'created', i.e. 'moon‑made' or 'moon‑like' — an epithet describing her beauty; آفرید is the past participial/verb form used adjectivally after the noun.
segments.s336-g54: که begins the relative/explanatory clause 'for/that'. چون is a comparative 'like/as'; او is the third‑person pronoun 'her'; کس in this negative context means 'no one' (i.e. nobody comparable). اندر زمانه is the adverbial 'in the age/era'. ندید is past 'saw' used here in a negative sense '(no one) has been seen' — the whole clause asserts that no one like her has appeared in the age.
segments.s336-g55: همه is the universal subject 'all/everyone'. خاک دارند is 'have earth' where دارند is the present‑tense third‑person plural 'they have/possess'. بالین و خشت are the objects listing what they have — 'pillow' and 'mudbrick' (i.e. humble bedding). The verb دارند is explicit here, unlike earlier elliptical lines.
segments.s336-g56: ندانم is the first‑person present negative 'I do not know' (ن‑ prefix + دانم). به is the preposition 'in/at/to'; دوزخ is 'hell'. درند appears here as an archaic/predictive copular/verb form meaning roughly 'are/lie (there)' in the context 'are in hell', but the provided sense list does not give a direct match, so it is best treated as an archaic locative/verb usage. ار is an old connective introducing the alternative 'or/whether' (not modern یا); بهشت is 'paradise/heaven'. The clause expresses uncertainty whether those people are in Hell or in Heaven.
segments.s336-g9: همی گفت is an iterative/continuous past: 'he kept saying' (همی signals repeated or ongoing action, گفت = said). کاجی من places an adjective/noun (کاجی ‘pine‑like’ / ‘a pine’) before the pronoun من in a poetic inversion: it functions as a hypothetical/predicative description of the speaker ('I, being a pine') or a simile; the clause sets up the speaker’s imagined condition. این انجمن is the direct object phrase 'this assembly/gathering' that the following verb will act upon.
segments.s336-g10: توانستمی is an archaic past 1st‑singular form 'I could' (توانستم with older spelling/pronunciation). برد here is the verbal root of بردن 'to carry/take' (used without the infinitival -ن in poetry) and combines with توانستمی to give 'I was able to take/carry'. با خویشتن is an adverbial phrase 'with myself' / 'away with me', with خویشتن an emphatic reflexive 'self'. The whole clause completes the speaker’s hypothetical: 'I could have carried this assembly away with me.'
segments.s336-g11: خروشی is a noun 'a cry/roar' (the -ی form used as an abstract noun). برآمد is a past intransitive 'arose/issued forth'. ز introduces the source 'from', and ایران سپاه is an inverted genitive: 'the host/army of Iran' (ز + [country] + [group] = 'from the Iranian army').
segments.s336-g12: که is a relative/conjunction 'that'. خورشید is the subject 'the sun'. بر چرخ is a prepositional phrase 'on the wheel/sky' (بر = on, چرخ = heaven/sky). گم کرد راه is a verbal cluster: گم (adjective/participle 'lost') appears before کرد (light verb 'to do/make/cause') so the sequence means 'caused to be lost' or idiomatically 'lost its way' — راه (way) is the object. The poetic order places the adjective before the light verb.
segments.s336-g13: پس is a discourse particle 'then/after that'. پرده‌ها (plural) 'the curtains/veils' is the first item in a list of people/objects who leave or appear. کودک خرد و زن is a compact poetic list: کودک 'child', خرد here functions as 'the wise' (خرد used as a substantive meaning 'wise/wise ones') and زن 'women'; the nouns are coordinated in a catalogic fashion typical of epic diction.
segments.s336-g14: به کوی و به بازار uses به + place twice for emphasis: 'to/into the lane and into the bazaar'. شد is the past of شدن 'became' / 'happened' and here introduces the result: انجمن (assembly/meeting) is the resulting state — 'an assembly came to be' or 'an assembly formed' in the streets and market.
segments.s336-g15: خروشیدن is the infinitive/verbal noun 'a roaring/wailing' used as a noun phrase. ناله و آه are nouns 'moan(s) and sigh(s)' coordinated after it; خاست (past of خاستن) is the verb 'arose/began' — so literally 'a wailing, moan and sigh arose.' This is a common poetic sequence where the verbal noun introduces the sound and خاست gives the action.
segments.s336-g16: به هر برزنی is a locative phrase 'in/to every barzan(ī)' (برزنی here = plain/threshing‑floor/village square — a common epic locale); ماتم شاه is a genitival phrase 'mourning of the king' (poetic order without an explicit ezāfe), and خاست repeats 'arose/began' — 'mourning for the king arose in every place.'
segments.s336-g89: هر آن گه که is an idiomatic temporal phrase meaning “whenever/at the time that.” دانِ گِه-clause follows: باشی is a present/subjunctive verb form (2nd sing.) meaning “you are” (classical/poetic order places the verb before its nominal predicate). تن آسان ز رنج literally “body (is) easy/from suffering” = “your body is free from hardship”; ز is the poetic preposition “from.”
segments.s336-g90: ننازی is the negative present/prohibitive formed by the negative prefix ن‑ + present stem (here addressing 2nd person): “do not be proud / do not boast.” The preposition به marks the target (تاج, crown; گنج, treasure). The verb repeats for the two objects, giving a parallel prohibition: don’t boast about the crown, and don’t boast about the treasure.
segments.s336-g91: چنان دان is an imperative/command (دان here is archaic/poetic imperative of دانستن used as “consider/think”): “consider it thus.” که introduces the content clause. رفتنت is the verbal noun + 2nd‑person suffix meaning “your going,” often euphemistically “your death.” نزدیک شد is past (شد = became) meaning “has become near / has drawn near.”
segments.s336-g92: This line means “the road to God for you became narrow.” به یزدان = “to/unto God.” ترا is realized as two morphemes in the line (ت = 2nd‑person bound pronoun, را = object/topic marker) placed before راه; together they function as “for you”/“to you.” راه باریک شد = “the way/road became narrow” (شد = became). The word order (pronominal cluster before راه) is a poetic placement of the clitic.
segments.s336-g93: همه داد جوی is a compound-like phrase: همه “all/everyone” + داد جوی (“seek justice” – داد = justice, جوی here = seeking/search), so “seek justice for all.” و connects the clauses. همه داد کن uses داد + the imperative کن (“do/make”) to mean “render/do justice to all.” The second clause is an imperative addressed to the ruler.
segments.s336-g94: ز گیتی = “from/out of the world” (ز is the poetic form of از). تن مهتر is the noun phrase “the body (تن) of the chief/foremost (مهتر)”—poetic genitive/attributive order. آزاد کن is the imperative “set free / liberate.” The whole is an instruction: free the eminent person (or free the body) from worldly bonds.
segments.s336-g95: فرود آمد is a verbal phrase meaning “he descended / alighted” (past). از introduces the origin: از باره لهراسب = “from Lohrasp’s barah.” باره here is poetic/archaic (literally “load/burden” or vehicle in context), and لهراسب is the proper name. زود is an adverb “quickly/soon,” placed sentence-final for emphasis.
segments.s336-g96: زمین را ببوسید is a straightforward past transitive clause: زمین “the ground” + را (definite object marker) + ببوسید “he kissed.” و links the next verb. شادی نمود is a common collocation meaning “(he) showed/displayed joy” (نمود is past of نمودن, “showed”), i.e., “he rejoiced.”
segments.s336-g25: بگفت is a past tense verb 'he said' (classical/poetic form). این is the demonstrative 'this' referring to what was just said. و simply links clauses ('and/then'). از + پایگه ('from the پایگه') marks source/place: پایگه here means 'station/headquarters'. اسب خواست is a past clause 'asked/wanted a horse' (خواست = 'wanted/asked' in past); word order is subject-verb-object condensed into verbs at the end as is normal in older Persian poetry.
segments.s336-g26: ز (poetic form of از) = 'from'. لشکرگه = 'army camp' (place from which something comes). آواز فریاد is a noun phrase 'a call/cry' (آواز here = 'call/voice' combined with فریاد 'shout/cry'). خاست is the past of خاستن 'to arise' → '(a cry) arose.' Note verb-final order: the verbal predicate خاست comes at the end.
segments.s336-g27: بیامد is past 'he came'. به ایوان شاهی is a prepositional phrase 'to the royal iwan/portico' (ایوان = porch/iwan, شاهی = 'royal'). دژم is an adjective 'sullen/grim' placed after the phrase as a postpositive descriptive word (typical in poetry).
segments.s336-g28: به آزاد سروندر آورده خم is a compact descriptive clause. به آزاد سرو = 'like the noble (آزاد) cypress (سرو)' (a common poetic simile for a tall, noble posture). اندر آورده خم is a perfective/resultative construction: آورده (brought) + خم (bend) with اندر 'in/within' → 'having brought into a bend inward' i.e. 'bent inward.' The whole phrase describes his posture (his tall figure bent inward).
segments.s336-g29: کنیزک is the noun 'little maid/young handmaid' (topic). بدش چار is an archaic compressed phrase: چار = 'remedy' and ش is the possessive 'his/her', so بدش چار here functions as 'a remedy for him' or 'his remedy' (بد is an older/poetic element fused into the phrase). چون آفتاب is a simile 'like the sun', describing her appearance or brilliance. The whole short phrase is elliptical and poetic: 'the little maid, his remedy, like the sun.'
segments.s336-g30: ندیدی is a past negative form 'had not seen' (classical narrative negative). کسی in this negative context means 'anyone/no one' (indefinite in affirmative but rendered as 'no one' with the negation). چهر ایشان = 'their face' (چهر = face/appearance; ایشان = they/them or polite 'their'), and به خواب = 'in a dream' (خواب here = 'dream'). Word order preserves the negative-verb phrase followed by the (indefinite) subject 'کسی' as in older Persian: 'no one had seen their face even in a dream.'
segments.s336-g31: ز پرده = 'from/behind the curtain' (ز is poetic از). بتان را is the definite object marked by را; بتان here is used poetically for 'beauties/beloveds' rather than literal idols. بر خویش = 'to himself' (بر = toward/to; خویش = himself — written as خوی + ش), and خواند is the past verb 'called/summoned' (خواند here = 'to call/summon' rather than 'read'). The clause literally: 'he summoned the beauties from behind the curtain to himself.'
segments.s336-g32: همه راز دل = 'all the secrets of the heart' (همه = all, راز = secret, دل = heart). پیش ایشان = 'before them/in their presence' (پیش = before/in front of). براند is a poetic perfect/past form of راندن with an initial ب-; here it functions idiomatically as 'puts forth/reveals' (poetically 'brings out'), so the clause means 'he laid bare/revealed all the secrets of his heart before them.' Word order places the verb at the end as is typical.
segments.s336-g121: گر begins a conditional clause ('if'). از + لشکر is a prepositional phrase 'from the army' (agent/source). آزار is a noun 'harm/injury' that is the object of داشتن; داری is 2nd-person present 'you have/suffer' (classical present used for a general/continuing fact). همی is an archaic/poetic present-continuous/emphatic particle reinforcing the present sense.
segments.s336-g122: مر = 'to me' (dative pronoun) marks the one affected. این تاج را is the definite object 'this crown' (را is the object marker). خوار is an adjective/participial idea 'despised/worthless'. داری here (again 2nd-person present) applies to the whole predicate 'you hold/treat (it) as...', and همی again is the archaic present/emphatic particle. Word order places the dative before the object for emphasis.
segments.s336-g123: بگوی here is an imperative/hortative 'say' (classical form; the provided sense list did not include the verbal imperative). و is the coordinating 'and/then'. تو is the vocative/subject 'you'. از گاه ایران is a prepositional phrase 'from the seat/place of Iran' (گاه here = seat/place/throne in context). مرو is the negative imperative 'do not go/leave' — so the clause reads 'and you, do not leave Iran’s seat.'
segments.s336-g124: جهان کهن را is the definite object 'the ancient world' (را marking definiteness/emphasis). مکن is the prohibitive 'do not (make/perform)'. شاه نو at the end is a vocative addressing the addressee 'O new king' — classical Persian often places vocatives after the clause. The line therefore warns the new king not to 'remake/alter' the ancient world (ellipsis: 'do not make [it] new/alter it').
segments.s336-g125: همه خاک باشیم is an optative/subjunctive wish 'may we all be dust' (باشیم is the jussive/subjunctive 'let us be/may we be'). اسب ترا is 'your horse' formed by اسب + 2nd-person possessive suffix (-ت) (written here as separate ت) and followed by را (object/definiteness marker). The verb باشیم is understood to govern the following object phrases as parallel wishes.
segments.s336-g126: پرستنده آذرگشسب is a noun phrase 'worshipper/devotee of Azar-Gashasp' (پرستنده + name). The 2nd-person suffix ت (written separately) makes it 'your worshipper of Azar-Gashasp' and را again marks definiteness/object; the verb باشیم from the previous segment is implied here as well ('may we be [this]').
segments.s336-g127: کجا شد is a past-tense interrogative 'where has ... gone/what became of ...' (شد = became/happened). ترا here is the 2nd-person possessive/dative ('your' / 'to you') attached after شد in classical word order. دانش و رای و هوش are a coordinated list 'knowledge and counsel and intelligence' — they are the items whose fate is being questioned.
segments.s336-g128: که is the relative/conjunctive 'that/which'. نزد فریدون = 'to/in the presence of Fereydun' (نزد used as 'in the presence of'). نیامد is simple past negative 'did not come'. سروش is the proper name 'Soroush' (the divine messenger/angel); the clause says the Soroush did not come to Fereydun, giving the reason for asking where those qualities have gone.
segments.s336-g65: نباید که is an impersonal modal: 'it should not / must not be that'. یزدان is the subject (God). چو here means 'when'. خواندت is an archaic/poetic form: خواند + ت (3rd‑person past verb with the enclitic '‑ت' = 'you'), so literally 'he called/summoned you' (rendered here as 'summons you' for natural English). پیش = 'in the presence of / before (Him)'. The whole segment sets up a subordinate time/clause ('It should not be that when God calls you before Him ...').
segments.s336-g66: روان شرم دارد is a simple clause: روان ('the soul/spirit') + شرم ('shame') as its complement + دارد ('has' — present/perspective auxiliary meaning 'is afflicted with / feels'). ز here is the poetic preposition 'because of / from'. کردار خویش is a genitive phrase: کردار ('deed/action') + خویش (possessive made from خوی + ش, together 'his/its own'), so 'ashamed because of its own deeds.'
segments.s336-g67: چو = 'when'. بینی is the 2nd‑person present/subjunctive form 'you see'. م + را here represent the object 'مرا' split into clitics (me + object marker), so the whole phrase means 'you see me'. با سیاوش به هم = 'together/with Siyavash' (با = with, سیاوش the name, به هم = 'together'). Word order is standard: temporal clause (when ...) + object phrase.
segments.s336-g68: ز شرم = 'because of shame' (ز = from/because of). دو خسرو here reads 'both (or two) kings' (دو = two/both, خسرو = king/Khosrow), serving as the subject of the following verb. بمانی is the 2nd‑person singular present/subjunctive or optative form 'you remain / you will remain' (used here in a future/optative sense). دژم is an adjective 'sullen / grim' describing the subject. The clause therefore says that, out of shame, the (two) kings will remain sullen.
segments.s336-g69: پذیرفت is a past perfect/narrative 3rd‑person singular 'accepted'. لهراسب is the proper name 'Lohrasp' (the subject who performed the accepting). ز + و (here contracted as 'زو' in the running text) means 'from him' (ز = from, و representing 'او' = him). هرچه گفت is an object clause 'whatever he said' (هرچه = whatever, گفت = said). The whole segment means 'Lohrasp accepted from him whatever he said.'
segments.s336-g70: که introduces a subordinate explanatory clause 'that / for'. با دیده‌شان = 'in/with their eyes' (با here marks the relation to 'their sight', and دیده‌شان = 'their eyes' with the possessive pronoun 'شان'). دارم is 1st‑person present 'I have' (here used with a perfective sense of possession/holding). اندر نهفت is a compound where اندر = 'in/within' and نهفت (from نهفتن) = 'hidden, concealed' (past participle/adj), so دارم اندر نهفت = 'I have (it) hidden (away)'. Together: 'that I had kept it hidden from their sight.'
segments.s336-g71: وز here is a poetic variant of از meaning 'from' (classical Persian often uses وز/وزن as prepositional forms). آن جایگه = 'that place/position' (آن = that, جایگه = place/position). تنگ بسته = a compound adjective 'narrow and closed/sealed' (تنگ = narrow, بسته = closed). میان = 'in the middle / midst'. The whole phrase gives the origin/location: 'from that narrow, sealed spot in the midst...'.
segments.s336-g72: بگردید is a 3rd‑person plural past/preterite or narrative-form verb from گشتن/گردیدن meaning 'they turned / they moved about' (classical narrative uses forms that look like present/subjunctive in modern Persian). بر گرد = 'around' (بر = on/upon/toward + گرد = around/about). ایرانیان = 'the Iranians' (object/complement of the motion). So this clause means 'they turned about around the Iranians.'
